metadata.title: Why the Bacteria That Cause Ulcers Also Cause Cancer

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The point that H. pylori causes cancer not through CagA as a classic oncogene but through decades of inflammation, reactive oxygen species, and compensatory regeneration explains both the risk and its rarity. I want to trace the slow progression. Can you (1) explain how chronic ROS damage plus elevated cell division compounds mutation accumulation over 30 to 50 years, (2) describe what tips only a minority through the gastritis-to-adenocarcinoma sequence, and (3) tell me whether eradicating the bacterium late still lowers cancer risk?

props.command: The point that H. pylori causes cancer not through CagA as a classic oncogene but through decades of inflammation, reactive oxygen species, and compensatory regeneration explains both the risk and its rarity. I want to trace the slow progression. props.segment: Why the Bacteria That Cause Ulcers

beats.title: Title card
beats.narration_text: A bacterium living in the stomach causes cancer — not by carrying a cancer gene, but by being too persistent to ignore.

viz.headline: Why Ulcer Bacteria Also Cause Cancer

beats.title: Stomach colonized — silent, indefinite

props.headline: Half the world carries H. pylori in the stomach lining. Most…

beats.narration_text: Half the world carries H. pylori in the stomach lining. Most feel nothing. A few, decades later, develop gastric cancer.

viz.description: Microscopy-style cross-section of stomach mucosa with spiral bacteria colonizing the epithelial surface. Newsprint treatment, cool clinical palette.

beats.title: The question — curable bacteria, fatal cancer
beats.narration_text: H. pylori is a bacterium — curable with one week of antibiotics. Yet it causes gastric cancer after 30 to 50 years. Most infected people never develop cancer at all. Why does a curable bacterial infection lead to cancer in some people after decades?

viz.question: Why does a curable bacterial infection cause cancer after 30-50 years?

beats.title: The naive expectation — search for an oncogene
beats.narration_text: The obvious model: bacteria cause cancer by encoding an oncogene — a protein that directly hijacks a cell's controls. H. pylori does inject CagA into cells. But CagA is not a classical transforming protein. The direct-oncogene model fails.

viz.description: Two-column layout: left column (TEAL, labeled DIRECT ONCOGENE) shows a pathogen injecting a protein arrow straight into a cancer-driver node — clean, one-step. Right column (CRIMSON, labeled H. PYLORI) shows the same expectation crossed out — CagA arrives but no cancer driver lights up.

beats.title: Why most infected people never get cancer
beats.narration_text: And there is a second puzzle. If the bacterium carried an oncogene, most carriers would develop cancer. They do not. The mechanism must explain both the cancer risk and its rarity.

viz.description: A grid of 100 dots representing H. pylori carriers. The vast majority are TEAL (no cancer). A small cluster at the corner is CRIMSON (cancer). Label: 'most carriers: no cancer'.

beats.title: Persistent colonization — the immune engine runs
beats.narration_text: H. pylori colonizes and stays. The immune system responds — neutrophils, macrophages — generating reactive oxygen and nitrogen species that constantly damage gastric epithelial DNA. Not once. Continuously. For decades.

viz.description: Central bacterium node (CRIMSON) with continuous arrows flowing to immune cells, then ROS/RNS arrows striking a DNA strand. A looping feedback arrow shows the cycle is ongoing — no endpoint. CRIMSON throughout.

beats.title: Compensatory regeneration — elevated cell division
beats.narration_text: Damaged cells must be replaced. Compensatory regeneration drives elevated cell division. More divisions mean more replication, more opportunity for replication errors. Mutations accumulate.

viz.description: A horizontal bar representing the epithelium. Small CRIMSON marks (mutations) accumulate along it with each beat. Below: a cell-division counter ticking upward. The bar fills left to right.

beats.title: The Correa cascade — geological strata
beats.narration_text: Over 30 to 50 years the stomach progresses through a fixed sequence: active gastritis, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, dysplasia, then adenocarcinoma. Each stage is laid down by ongoing inflammatory pressure — not a single event.

viz.description: A horizontal timeline spanning five decades. Five stages appear left to right as geological strata, each a labeled stratum: Gastritis (CRIMSON light), Atrophy, Metaplasia, Dysplasia, Adenocarcinoma (deep CRIMSON). A bacterium icon sits at the far left as the low-grade engine. The strata accumulate one at a time.

beats.title: Eradication interrupts the engine
beats.narration_text: Remove the bacterium with antibiotics and the inflammatory engine stops. Long-term data: eradicating H. pylori reduces gastric cancer incidence by roughly 35 percent — not because a cancer gene was removed, but because the mutagenic pressure ended.

viz.description: Two horizontal arms side by side. Left (TEAL): eradication arrow cuts the engine at year 1, cascade halts at Gastritis, endpoint shows low cancer risk. Right (CRIMSON): no eradication, cascade progresses through all five stages to Adenocarcinoma. Label the 35% reduction on the left arm.

beats.title: Why only some — cofactors and latency
beats.narration_text: Most carriers never develop cancer because the mechanism is probabilistic — cumulative mutagenic pressure, not a guaranteed transformation. Host genetics, bacterial strain, and timing all modulate who crosses the threshold.

viz.description: A single horizontal line representing the mutation threshold for cancer. Two populations of dots (TEAL = no cancer, CRIMSON = cancer). Dots spread around the line — most fall below, a few cross. No single deterministic arrow — the crossing is probabilistic.

beats.title: Two villages — one eradicates, one does not
beats.narration_text: Two villages in rural Japan. High H. pylori prevalence in both. Village A runs an eradication program in the 1990s. Village B does not. By 2020: gastric cancer incidence in Village A is 40 percent lower than Village B. Not genetics, not diet — the presence or absence of a 30-year inflammatory engine. Illustrative.

viz.description: Two side-by-side bar charts. Left (TEAL): Village A — bar starts high in 1990 (pre-eradication), then drops steeply at 2000, low by 2020. Right (CRIMSON): Village B — bar stays elevated all the way to 2020. Label: 40% lower on the left. Both bars show the same starting prevalence. Illustrative.

beats.title: Endcard
beats.narration_text: A bacterium causes cancer not by carrying an oncogene, but by persisting long enough to run a decades-long inflammatory engine that does all the mutagenic work. Turn off the engine with antibiotics, and you turn off the cancer risk.

viz.question: Why does a curable bacterial infection cause cancer after 30-50 years?
viz.answer: Persistent colonization sustains a chronic immune response that generates DNA-damaging reactive species for decades — the inflammation, not the bacterium, assembles the driver mutations.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Remove the bacterium with antibiotics and the inflammatory engine stops. Long-term data: eradicating H. pylori reduces gastric cancer incidence by roughly 35 percent — not because a cancer gene was removed, but because the mutagenic pressure ended. Most carriers never develop cancer because the mechanism is probabilistic — cumulative mutagenic pressure, not a guaranteed transformation. Host genetics, bacterial strain, and timing all modulate who crosses the threshold.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why the Bacteria That Cause Ulcers Also Cause Cancer] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
